OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation Table

Generalized Wasserstein Distances

The first family of extensions keeps the idea of a distance between measures, but changes the geometry used to compare them. The variants in this chapter relax mass conservation, reduce high-dimensional transport to one-dimensional projections, or replace the trace quadratic cost by spectral gauges and robust projected viewpoints.

These constructions are useful when the standard distance Wp\Wass_pWp​ is too rigid or too expensive. They preserve much of the metric intuition of optimal transport, but expose new controls: how expensive it is to delete mass, which projections should be trusted, and which directions of displacement should be penalized.

Guiding Comparison

Balanced Wasserstein fixes the marginals exactly. Unbalanced OT relaxes the marginals. Sliced OT compares many one-dimensional shadows. Linear OT embeds measures through maps from a fixed reference. Spectral OT changes the scalar quadratic cost into a gauge of the whole displacement covariance.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Unbalanced OT¶

Unbalanced OT allows mass creation and destruction by penalizing marginal mismatch. It is essential when histograms are not normalized, when observations contain outliers, or when only part of the source should match the target Liero et al., 2018Chizat et al., 2018Chizat et al., 2018.

Relaxed Formulation¶

For nonnegative measures (α,β)∈M+(X)×M+(Y)(\alpha,\beta)\in\mathcal M_+(\X)\times\mathcal M_+(\Y)(α,β)∈M+​(X)×M+​(Y), a generic relaxed formulation is

UWc(α,β)=inf⁡π∈M+(X×Y)∫X×Yc(x,y) dπ(x,y)+Dψ1(π1∣α)+Dψ2(π2∣β),\mathsf{UW}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\pi\in\mathcal M_+(\X\times\Y)} \int_{\X\times\Y} c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y) + \mathcal D_{\psi_1}(\pi_1\mid\alpha) + \mathcal D_{\psi_2}(\pi_2\mid\beta),UWc​(α,β)=π∈M+​(X×Y)inf​∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ(x,y)+Dψ1​​(π1​∣α)+Dψ2​​(π2​∣β),
(1)

where ψ1,ψ2\psi_1,\psi_2ψ1​,ψ2​ are convex entropy functions. Exact conservation (π1,π2)=(α,β)(\pi_1,\pi_2)=(\alpha,\beta)(π1​,π2​)=(α,β) is replaced by a cost for changing the marginals. Writing ψs=τψˉs\psi_s=\tau\bar\psi_sψs​=τψˉ​s​ exposes the relaxation scale:

UWc,τ(α,β)=inf⁡π≥0∫c dπ+τDψˉ1(π1∣α)+τDψˉ2(π2∣β).\mathsf{UW}_{c,\tau}(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\pi\geq0} \int c\d\pi + \tau\mathcal D_{\bar\psi_1}(\pi_1\mid\alpha) + \tau\mathcal D_{\bar\psi_2}(\pi_2\mid\beta).UWc,τ​(α,β)=π≥0inf​∫cdπ+τDψˉ​1​​(π1​∣α)+τDψˉ​2​​(π2​∣β).
(2)

Large τ\tauτ makes marginal mismatch expensive and approaches balanced OT when the total masses are compatible. Small τ\tauτ makes creation and destruction cheap; after rescaling by τ\tauτ, the zero-transport part reveals the pure divergence geometry.

Proposition: Small-Transport-Scale Limit

Assume that α,β\alpha,\betaα,β are finite measures on a compact metric space X\XX, that ccc is continuous, c≥0c\geq0c≥0, and c(x,y)=0c(x,y)=0c(x,y)=0 if and only if x=yx=yx=y. Assume also that the marginal divergences are nonnegative, weak-* lower semicontinuous, and have weak-* compact sublevel sets on M+(X)\mathcal M_+(\X)M+​(X). Then

lim⁡τ↓01τUWc,τ(α,β)=inf⁡ρ∈M+(X)Dψˉ1(ρ∣α)+Dψˉ2(ρ∣β).\lim_{\tau\downarrow0} \frac{1}{\tau}\mathsf{UW}_{c,\tau}(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\rho\in\mathcal M_+(\X)} \mathcal D_{\bar\psi_1}(\rho\mid\alpha) + \mathcal D_{\bar\psi_2}(\rho\mid\beta).τ↓0lim​τ1​UWc,τ​(α,β)=ρ∈M+​(X)inf​Dψˉ​1​​(ρ∣α)+Dψˉ​2​​(ρ∣β).
(3)

The right-hand side is the infimal gluing divergence obtained by matching the two measures through a common zero-transport marginal ρ\rhoρ. In the dominated case, if α=aλ\alpha=a\lambdaα=aλ, β=bλ\beta=b\lambdaβ=bλ, and ρ=rλ\rho=r\lambdaρ=rλ, this decouples pointwise:

∫mψˉ1,ψˉ2(a(x),b(x)) dλ(x),mψˉ1,ψˉ2(a,b):=inf⁡r≥0a ψˉ1(r/a)+b ψˉ2(r/b),\int \mathfrak m_{\bar\psi_1,\bar\psi_2}(a(x),b(x))\d\lambda(x), \qquad \mathfrak m_{\bar\psi_1,\bar\psi_2}(a,b) \eqdef \inf_{r\geq0} a\,\bar\psi_1(r/a)+b\,\bar\psi_2(r/b),∫mψˉ​1​,ψˉ​2​​(a(x),b(x))dλ(x),mψˉ​1​,ψˉ​2​​(a,b):=r≥0inf​aψˉ​1​(r/a)+bψˉ​2​(r/b),
(4)

with the usual recession conventions when a=0a=0a=0 or b=0b=0b=0. For KL marginal penalties,

inf⁡ρ∈M+(X)KL⁡(ρ∣α)+KL⁡(ρ∣β)=∫(a−b)2 dλ.\inf_{\rho\in\mathcal M_+(\X)} \operatorname{KL}(\rho\mid\alpha) + \operatorname{KL}(\rho\mid\beta) = \int (\sqrt a-\sqrt b)^2\d\lambda .ρ∈M+​(X)inf​KL(ρ∣α)+KL(ρ∣β)=∫(a​−b​)2dλ.
(5)

Thus KL marginal relaxation contains the squared Hellinger distance as its local mass-variation limit.

Proof

For the upper bound, restrict to diagonal plans π=(Id,Id)♯ρ\pi=(\Id,\Id)_\sharp\rhoπ=(Id,Id)♯​ρ, whose transport cost is zero and whose two marginals are both ρ\rhoρ. This gives the desired upper bound after optimizing over ρ\rhoρ.

For the lower bound, let τn↓0\tau_n\downarrow0τn​↓0 and let πn\pi_nπn​ be almost minimizing plans with bounded scaled values τn−1UWc,τn(α,β)\tau_n^{-1}\mathsf{UW}_{c,\tau_n}(\alpha,\beta)τn−1​UWc,τn​​(α,β). Since the divergences are nonnegative, ∫c dπn=O(τn)\int c\d\pi_n=O(\tau_n)∫cdπn​=O(τn​), hence ∫c dπn→0\int c\d\pi_n\to0∫cdπn​→0. The bounded scaled values also put the two marginals in compact divergence sublevel sets. Since a coupling has the same total mass as each marginal, the couplings are tight on X×X\X\times\XX×X. Up to subsequences, πn⇀π0\pi_n\rightharpoonup\pi_0πn​⇀π0​.

Lower semicontinuity of the transport cost yields ∫c dπ0=0\int c\d\pi_0=0∫cdπ0​=0, so π0\pi_0π0​ is concentrated on the diagonal. Its two marginals are therefore equal to a common measure ρ\rhoρ. Lower semicontinuity of the marginal divergences gives

lim inf⁡n1τnUWc,τn(α,β)≥Dψˉ1(ρ∣α)+Dψˉ2(ρ∣β),\liminf_n \frac{1}{\tau_n} \mathsf{UW}_{c,\tau_n}(\alpha,\beta) \geq \mathcal D_{\bar\psi_1}(\rho\mid\alpha) + \mathcal D_{\bar\psi_2}(\rho\mid\beta),nliminf​τn​1​UWc,τn​​(α,β)≥Dψˉ​1​​(ρ∣α)+Dψˉ​2​​(ρ∣β),
(6)

and optimizing over ρ\rhoρ gives the lower bound.

In the dominated case, the minimization over ρ=rλ\rho=r\lambdaρ=rλ decouples into the scalar envelope mψˉ1,ψˉ2\mathfrak m_{\bar\psi_1,\bar\psi_2}mψˉ​1​,ψˉ​2​​. For KL, no singular part is admissible when α\alphaα and β\betaβ are dominated by λ\lambdaλ. The pointwise objective is rlog⁡(r/a)−r+a+rlog⁡(r/b)−r+br\log(r/a)-r+a+r\log(r/b)-r+brlog(r/a)−r+a+rlog(r/b)−r+b. Its optimality condition is log⁡(r/a)+log⁡(r/b)=0\log(r/a)+\log(r/b)=0log(r/a)+log(r/b)=0, hence r=abr=\sqrt{ab}r=ab​, and the minimum is a+b−2ab=(a−b)2a+b-2\sqrt{ab}=(\sqrt a-\sqrt b)^2a+b−2ab​=(a​−b​)2.

Proposition: Dual of Unbalanced Optimal Transport

Under the usual Fenchel--Rockafellar qualification assumptions, the relaxed primal value in (1) equals

UWc(α,β)=sup⁡f⊕g≤c−Dψ1∗(−f∣α)−Dψ2∗(−g∣β).\mathsf{UW}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \sup_{f\oplus g\leq c} - \mathcal D_{\psi_1}^*(-f\mid\alpha) - \mathcal D_{\psi_2}^*(-g\mid\beta).UWc​(α,β)=f⊕g≤csup​−Dψ1​∗​(−f∣α)−Dψ2​∗​(−g∣β).
(7)
Proof

Use the variational formula for the dual of a divergence and introduce the marginal variables through continuous potentials:

inf⁡π≥0sup⁡f,g∫c dπ+∫−f dπ1+∫−g dπ2−Dψ1∗(−f∣α)−Dψ2∗(−g∣β).\inf_{\pi\geq0}\sup_{f,g} \int c\d\pi + \int -f\d\pi_1 + \int -g\d\pi_2 - \mathcal D_{\psi_1}^*(-f\mid\alpha) - \mathcal D_{\psi_2}^*(-g\mid\beta).π≥0inf​f,gsup​∫cdπ+∫−fdπ1​+∫−gdπ2​−Dψ1​∗​(−f∣α)−Dψ2​∗​(−g∣β).
(8)

Exchanging the infimum and supremum gives

sup⁡f,g−Dψ1∗(−f∣α)−Dψ2∗(−g∣β)+inf⁡π≥0∫(c−(f⊕g)) dπ.\sup_{f,g} - \mathcal D_{\psi_1}^*(-f\mid\alpha) - \mathcal D_{\psi_2}^*(-g\mid\beta) + \inf_{\pi\geq0} \int \big(c-(f\oplus g)\big)\d\pi .f,gsup​−Dψ1​∗​(−f∣α)−Dψ2​∗​(−g∣β)+π≥0inf​∫(c−(f⊕g))dπ.
(9)

The last infimum is 0 when f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\leq cf⊕g≤c and −∞-\infty−∞ otherwise.

Reverse and Homogeneous Formulations¶

The Liero--Mielke--Savare formulation rewrites marginal penalties as a local transport cost and then homogenizes it. Assuming first that the reference measures and transported marginals have mutually absolutely continuous parts, one can factor the objective as

∫c(x,y) dπ(x,y)+Dψ1(π1∣α)+Dψ2(π2∣β)=∫(c(x,y)+ψ1 ⁣( dπ1 dα(x)) dα dπ1(x)+ψ2 ⁣( dπ2 dβ(y)) dβ dπ2(y)) dπ(x,y).\begin{aligned} &\int c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y) + \mathcal D_{\psi_1}(\pi_1\mid\alpha) + \mathcal D_{\psi_2}(\pi_2\mid\beta) \\ &\quad = \int \left( c(x,y) + \psi_1\!\left(\frac{\d\pi_1}{\d\alpha}(x)\right) \frac{\d\alpha}{\d\pi_1}(x) + \psi_2\!\left(\frac{\d\pi_2}{\d\beta}(y)\right) \frac{\d\beta}{\d\pi_2}(y) \right) \d\pi(x,y). \end{aligned}​∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y)+Dψ1​​(π1​∣α)+Dψ2​​(π2​∣β)=∫(c(x,y)+ψ1​(dαdπ1​​(x))dπ1​dα​(x)+ψ2​(dβdπ2​​(y))dπ2​dβ​(y))dπ(x,y).​
(10)

This motivates the local reverse cost

Lc(r,s):=c+rψ1(1/r)+sψ2(1/s),L_c(r,s) \eqdef c+r\psi_1(1/r)+s\psi_2(1/s),Lc​(r,s):=c+rψ1​(1/r)+sψ2​(1/s),
(11)

with the usual recession convention at r=0r=0r=0 or s=0s=0s=0. If α=Fπ1+α⊥\alpha=F\pi_1+\alpha^\perpα=Fπ1​+α⊥ and β=Gπ2+β⊥\beta=G\pi_2+\beta^\perpβ=Gπ2​+β⊥ are the Lebesgue decompositions of the reference marginals with respect to the transported marginals, then

UWc(α,β)=inf⁡π≥0∫Lc(x,y)(F(x),G(y)) dπ(x,y)+ψ1(0)α⊥(X)+ψ2(0)β⊥(Y).\mathsf{UW}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\pi\geq0} \int L_{c(x,y)}(F(x),G(y))\d\pi(x,y) + \psi_1(0)\alpha^\perp(\X) + \psi_2(0)\beta^\perp(\Y).UWc​(α,β)=π≥0inf​∫Lc(x,y)​(F(x),G(y))dπ(x,y)+ψ1​(0)α⊥(X)+ψ2​(0)β⊥(Y).
(12)

The homogeneous formulation is obtained by taking the perspective transform of LcL_cLc​,

Hc(r,s):=inf⁡θ>0θLc(r/θ,s/θ),H_c(r,s) \eqdef \inf_{\theta>0} \theta L_c(r/\theta,s/\theta),Hc​(r,s):=θ>0inf​θLc​(r/θ,s/θ),
(13)

which is positively 1-homogeneous. It defines

HWc(α,β)=inf⁡π≥0∫Hc(x,y)(F(x),G(y)) dπ(x,y)+ψ1(0)α⊥(X)+ψ2(0)β⊥(Y).\mathsf{HW}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\pi\geq0} \int H_{c(x,y)}(F(x),G(y))\d\pi(x,y) + \psi_1(0)\alpha^\perp(\X) + \psi_2(0)\beta^\perp(\Y).HWc​(α,β)=π≥0inf​∫Hc(x,y)​(F(x),G(y))dπ(x,y)+ψ1​(0)α⊥(X)+ψ2​(0)β⊥(Y).
(14)
Proposition: Homogenization Does Not Change the Cost

One has

HWc(α,β)=UWc(α,β).\mathsf{HW}_c(\alpha,\beta)=\mathsf{UW}_c(\alpha,\beta).HWc​(α,β)=UWc​(α,β).
(15)
Proof

The inequality HW≤UW\mathsf{HW}\leq\mathsf{UW}HW≤UW follows from Hc≤LcH_c\leq L_cHc​≤Lc​ by taking θ=1\theta=1θ=1. Conversely, take a feasible measure π\piπ in the homogeneous formulation. By definition of the perspective transform, for every (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) and every η>0\eta>0η>0 there exists a scale θ(x,y)>0\theta(x,y)>0θ(x,y)>0 such that

Hc(x,y)(F(x),G(y))+η≥θ(x,y)Lc(x,y)(F(x)/θ(x,y),G(y)/θ(x,y)).H_{c(x,y)}(F(x),G(y))+\eta \geq \theta(x,y) L_{c(x,y)} \big(F(x)/\theta(x,y),G(y)/\theta(x,y)\big).Hc(x,y)​(F(x),G(y))+η≥θ(x,y)Lc(x,y)​(F(x)/θ(x,y),G(y)/θ(x,y)).
(16)

Replacing π\piπ by the rescaled measure π~=θπ\tilde\pi=\theta\piπ~=θπ and the densities by F/θF/\thetaF/θ and G/θG/\thetaG/θ gives an admissible competitor for the reverse formulation with cost no larger than the homogeneous cost plus ηπ(X×Y)\eta\pi(\X\times\Y)ηπ(X×Y). Letting η→0\eta\to0η→0 yields UW≤HW\mathsf{UW}\leq\mathsf{HW}UW≤HW. The singular terms are unchanged because the same rescaling is performed before taking the Lebesgue decomposition of the marginals.

Conic Lifting¶

Assume now that X=Y\X=\YX=Y and ψ1=ψ2=ψ\psi_1=\psi_2=\psiψ1​=ψ2​=ψ. The homogeneous formulation lifts the problem to the cone space C[X]:=(X×R+)/∼\mathfrak C[\X]\eqdef(\X\times\RR_+)/\simC[X]:=(X×R+​)/∼, where all points (x,0)(x,0)(x,0) are identified at the apex. For an exponent p≥1p\geq1p≥1, define

D((x,r),(y,s)):=Hc(x,y)(rp,sp)1/p.\mathsf D((x,r),(y,s)) \eqdef H_{c(x,y)}(r^p,s^p)^{1/p}.D((x,r),(y,s)):=Hc(x,y)​(rp,sp)1/p.
(17)

Several classical unbalanced geometries are obtained by choosing ψ\psiψ, ccc and ppp so that D\mathsf DD is a distance on the cone:

  • Dψ=KL⁡\mathcal D_\psi=\operatorname{KL}Dψ​=KL, p=2p=2p=2, and c(x,y)=−log⁡cos⁡2(d(x,y)∧π/2)c(x,y)=-\log\cos^2(d(x,y)\wedge\pi/2)c(x,y)=−logcos2(d(x,y)∧π/2) give the Hellinger--Kantorovich or Wasserstein--Fisher--Rao cone metric

D((x,r),(y,s))2=r2+s2−2rscos⁡(d(x,y)∧π/2).\mathsf D((x,r),(y,s))^2 = r^2+s^2-2rs\cos(d(x,y)\wedge\pi/2).D((x,r),(y,s))2=r2+s2−2rscos(d(x,y)∧π/2).
(18)

  • Dψ=KL⁡\mathcal D_\psi=\operatorname{KL}Dψ​=KL, p=2p=2p=2, and c(x,y)=d(x,y)2c(x,y)=d(x,y)^2c(x,y)=d(x,y)2 give the Gaussian Hellinger cone metric

D((x,r),(y,s))2=r2+s2−2rse−d(x,y)2/2.\mathsf D((x,r),(y,s))^2 = r^2+s^2-2rs e^{-d(x,y)^2/2}.D((x,r),(y,s))2=r2+s2−2rse−d(x,y)2/2.
(19)

  • Dψ=TV\mathcal D_\psi=\TVDψ​=TV, p=1p=1p=1, and c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y)c(x,y)=d(x,y) give the partial-transport cone cost

D((x,r),(y,s))=r+s−(r∧s)(2−d(x,y))+.\mathsf D((x,r),(y,s)) = r+s-(r\wedge s)(2-d(x,y))_+.D((x,r),(y,s))=r+s−(r∧s)(2−d(x,y))+​.
(20)

The corresponding cone value is

CW(α,β)=inf⁡γ∈M+(C[X]2){∫D((x,r),(y,s))p dγ  ;  ∫rp dγ1(⋅,r)=α,∫sp dγ2(⋅,s)=β}.\mathsf{CW}(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\gamma\in\mathcal M_+(\mathfrak C[\X]^2)} \left\{ \int \mathsf D((x,r),(y,s))^p\d\gamma \; ; \; \int r^p\d\gamma_1(\cdot,r)=\alpha,\quad \int s^p\d\gamma_2(\cdot,s)=\beta \right\}.CW(α,β)=γ∈M+​(C[X]2)inf​{∫D((x,r),(y,s))pdγ;∫rpdγ1​(⋅,r)=α,∫spdγ2​(⋅,s)=β}.
(21)
Theorem: Cone Formulation of Unbalanced OT

One has UW=HW=CW\mathsf{UW}=\mathsf{HW}=\mathsf{CW}UW=HW=CW. If D\mathsf DD is a distance, then CW1/p\mathsf{CW}^{1/p}CW1/p is a distance between nonnegative measures.

Proof

The equality UW=HW\mathsf{UW}=\mathsf{HW}UW=HW is the homogenization proposition. To prove HW=CW\mathsf{HW}=\mathsf{CW}HW=CW, disintegrate an admissible cone coupling γ\gammaγ with respect to its spatial variables (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) and radii (r,s)(r,s)(r,s). The cone marginal constraints say precisely that the spatial marginals are recovered after weighting by rpr^prp and sps^psp. Since D((x,r),(y,s))p=Hc(x,y)(rp,sp)\mathsf D((x,r),(y,s))^p=H_{c(x,y)}(r^p,s^p)D((x,r),(y,s))p=Hc(x,y)​(rp,sp), integrating the cone cost gives the homogeneous objective. Conversely, any homogeneous competitor can be lifted to the cone by placing, over each (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), radii whose pppth powers are the two density factors appearing in HcH_cHc​.

If D\mathsf DD is a distance on the cone, then CW1/p\mathsf{CW}^{1/p}CW1/p is the usual ppp-Wasserstein distance between lifted measures under the linear cone-marginal constraints. Symmetry and the triangle inequality follow from the corresponding Wasserstein properties and the gluing lemma on the cone. If the distance is zero, an optimal cone coupling is concentrated on the diagonal of the cone, so the weighted projections agree and therefore α=β\alpha=\betaα=β.

Entropic KL Relaxation¶

A generic entropic regularization of unbalanced OT reads

inf⁡π∈M+(X×Y)∫c dπ+Dψ1(π1∣α)+Dψ2(π2∣β)+ϵDϕ(π∣α⊗β).\inf_{\pi\in\mathcal M_+(\X\times\Y)} \int c\d\pi + \mathcal D_{\psi_1}(\pi_1\mid\alpha) + \mathcal D_{\psi_2}(\pi_2\mid\beta) + \epsilon\mathcal D_\phi(\pi\mid\alpha\otimes\beta).π∈M+​(X×Y)inf​∫cdπ+Dψ1​​(π1​∣α)+Dψ2​​(π2​∣β)+ϵDϕ​(π∣α⊗β).
(22)

Its dual is

sup⁡f,g−Dψ1∗(−f∣α)−Dψ2∗(−g∣β)−ϵDϕ∗(f⊕g−cϵ|α⊗β).\sup_{f,g} - \mathcal D_{\psi_1}^*(-f\mid\alpha) - \mathcal D_{\psi_2}^*(-g\mid\beta) - \epsilon\mathcal D_\phi^* \left(\frac{f\oplus g-c}{\epsilon}\middle|\alpha\otimes\beta\right).f,gsup​−Dψ1​∗​(−f∣α)−Dψ2​∗​(−g∣β)−ϵDϕ∗​(ϵf⊕g−c​∣∣​α⊗β).
(23)

For Dϕ=KL⁡\mathcal D_\phi=\operatorname{KL}Dϕ​=KL, the primal-dual relation is  dπ=e(f⊕g−c)/ϵ dα dβ\d\pi=e^{(f\oplus g-c)/\epsilon}\d\alpha\d\betadπ=e(f⊕g−c)/ϵdαdβ. If in addition Dψ1=Dψ2=τKL⁡\mathcal D_{\psi_1}=\mathcal D_{\psi_2}=\tau\operatorname{KL}Dψ1​​=Dψ2​​=τKL, coordinate maximization gives the damped soft transforms

f(x)=−τϵτ+ϵlog⁡∫Yexp⁡(g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dβ(y),g(y)=−τϵτ+ϵlog⁡∫Xexp⁡(f(x)−c(x,y)ϵ) dα(x).\begin{aligned} f(x) &= - \frac{\tau\epsilon}{\tau+\epsilon} \log\int_\Y \exp\left(\frac{g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right)\d\beta(y),\\ g(y) &= - \frac{\tau\epsilon}{\tau+\epsilon} \log\int_\X \exp\left(\frac{f(x)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right)\d\alpha(x). \end{aligned}f(x)g(y)​=−τ+ϵτϵ​log∫Y​exp(ϵg(y)−c(x,y)​)dβ(y),=−τ+ϵτϵ​log∫X​exp(ϵf(x)−c(x,y)​)dα(x).​
(24)

In the discrete case, with Ki,j=e−Ci,j/ϵaibjK_{i,j}=e^{-C_{i,j}/\epsilon}a_i b_jKi,j​=e−Ci,j​/ϵai​bj​ and ρ=τ/(τ+ϵ)\rho=\tau/(\tau+\epsilon)ρ=τ/(τ+ϵ), this gives the generalized Sinkhorn scaling

ui←(ai(Kv)i)ρ,vj←(bj(K⊤u)j)ρ,P=diag⁡(u)Kdiag⁡(v).u_i\leftarrow \left(\frac{a_i}{(Kv)_i}\right)^\rho, \qquad v_j\leftarrow \left(\frac{b_j}{(K^\top u)_j}\right)^\rho, \qquad P=\diag(u)K\diag(v).ui​←((Kv)i​ai​​)ρ,vj​←((K⊤u)j​bj​​)ρ,P=diag(u)Kdiag(v).
(25)

The exponent ρ<1\rho<1ρ<1 is the visible difference with balanced Sinkhorn: marginal corrections are damped because violating the marginals is allowed.

show_book_figure("unbalanced-mass-relaxation")

KL unbalanced OT on one-dimensional Gaussian-mixture densities. The central matrix is the transported coupling. The side curves compare the prescribed marginals with the transported marginals; increasing τ\tauτ makes marginal mismatch more expensive, so more mass is moved rather than created or destroyed.

The interactive demo below exposes the two most important regularization scales. Increasing τ\tauτ pushes the transported marginals closer to the prescribed ones; increasing ϵ\epsilonϵ spreads the coupling itself.

Interactive panel. Use the deletion cost and regularization controls to see when unbalanced transport prefers moving mass, creating mass, or removing it.

The entropy used in the marginal relaxation also changes the qualitative behavior. A KL penalty leads to smooth multiplicative rescaling. The reverse-KL, or Burg, penalty blows up when a transported marginal vanishes where the prescribed marginal is positive, so it discourages complete deletion of small modes. Total variation has a linear kink and behaves closer to partial transport: mass is either kept active or created and destroyed at nearly constant marginal price.

show_book_figure("unbalanced-divergence-choice")

Effect of the marginal divergence in unbalanced entropic OT. The geometric cost, entropic plan regularization ϵ\epsilonϵ, and relaxation strength τ\tauτ are fixed; only the marginal penalty changes. KL allows smooth mass variation, Burg keeps transported marginals from vanishing on prescribed modes, and total variation gives a sharper active-mass selection.

Sliced Wasserstein Distances¶

Sliced Wasserstein trades exact high-dimensional geometry for many one-dimensional projections. It is cheap, differentiable after sorting, and often effective in imaging and learning. For measures on Rd\RR^dRd and θ∈Sd−1\theta\in\mathbb S^{d-1}θ∈Sd−1, let Pθ(x)=⟨θ,x⟩P_\theta(x)=\dotp{\theta}{x}Pθ​(x)=⟨θ,x⟩ be the projection on direction θ\thetaθ.

Definition: Sliced Wasserstein Distance

Let σ\sigmaσ be the uniform probability measure on the sphere Sd−1\mathbb S^{d-1}Sd−1. The sliced ppp-Wasserstein distance is

SW⁡p(α,β)p:=∫Sd−1Wp((Pθ)♯α,(Pθ)♯β)p dσ(θ).\operatorname{SW}_p(\alpha,\beta)^p \eqdef \int_{\mathbb S^{d-1}} \Wass_p\big((P_\theta)_\sharp\alpha,(P_\theta)_\sharp\beta\big)^p \d\sigma(\theta).SWp​(α,β)p:=∫Sd−1​Wp​((Pθ​)♯​α,(Pθ​)♯​β)pdσ(θ).
(26)

This construction is closely related to the Radon transform and is much cheaper to approximate numerically than high-dimensional OT, since each projected problem can be solved by sorting or quantiles Rabin et al., 2011Bonneel et al., 2015Kolouri et al., 2016. It metrizes the same weak-plus-moment topology as Wp\Wass_pWp​, but its geometry is not bi-Lipschitz equivalent to Wp\Wass_pWp​ in high dimension Nadjahi et al., 2019.

show_book_figure("sliced-wasserstein-projections")

Sliced Wasserstein projections between two planar densities. Fixed directions are drawn on both densities, and the middle panels show smoothed one-dimensional density estimates of the projected measures. Sliced OT averages one-dimensional Wasserstein discrepancies over many such directions.

The interactive demo separates two uses of a slice: comparing projected measures and lifting the sorted one-dimensional matching back to the plane. The lifted plan is always feasible in the original space, but it need not be the quadratic optimal plan.

Interactive panel. Use the projection angle and number of directions to see how sliced Wasserstein distances reduce high-dimensional transport to one-dimensional matchings.

Proposition: Metric Properties of Sliced Wasserstein

For p≥1p\geq1p≥1, SW⁡p\operatorname{SW}_pSWp​ is a distance on Pp(Rd)\mathcal P_p(\RR^d)Pp​(Rd). Moreover, SW⁡p\operatorname{SW}_pSWp​ metrizes weak convergence together with convergence of the pppth moment. Finally,

SW⁡p(α,β)≤Wp(α,β),\operatorname{SW}_p(\alpha,\beta)\leq\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta),SWp​(α,β)≤Wp​(α,β),
(27)

and, for p=2p=2p=2 with the uniform probability measure on the sphere,

SW⁡2(α,β)2≤1dW2(α,β)2.\operatorname{SW}_2(\alpha,\beta)^2 \leq \frac{1}{d}\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta)^2.SW2​(α,β)2≤d1​W2​(α,β)2.
(28)
Proof

Non-negativity and symmetry follow from the one-dimensional Wasserstein distance. For the triangle inequality, apply the triangle inequality of Wp\Wass_pWp​ for each direction θ\thetaθ and then Minkowski’s inequality in Lp(Sd−1)L^p(\mathbb S^{d-1})Lp(Sd−1).

If SW⁡p(α,β)=0\operatorname{SW}_p(\alpha,\beta)=0SWp​(α,β)=0, then (Pθ)♯α=(Pθ)♯β(P_\theta)_\sharp\alpha=(P_\theta)_\sharp\beta(Pθ​)♯​α=(Pθ​)♯​β for almost every direction. By continuity of characteristic functions this holds for all directions, and the Cramer--Wold theorem implies α=β\alpha=\betaα=β.

The bound SW⁡p≤Wp\operatorname{SW}_p\leq\Wass_pSWp​≤Wp​ follows because PθP_\thetaPθ​ is 1-Lipschitz. For p=2p=2p=2, using any coupling π\piπ between α\alphaα and β\betaβ,

∫Sd−1∫∣⟨x−y,θ⟩∣2 dπ(x,y) dσ(θ)=1d∫∥x−y∥2 dπ(x,y).\int_{\mathbb S^{d-1}}\int |\dotp{x-y}{\theta}|^2\d\pi(x,y)\d\sigma(\theta) = \frac{1}{d}\int \norm{x-y}^2\d\pi(x,y).∫Sd−1​∫∣⟨x−y,θ⟩∣2dπ(x,y)dσ(θ)=d1​∫∥x−y∥2dπ(x,y).
(29)

Optimizing over π\piπ gives the sharper inequality. The weak-convergence statement follows from the same Cramer--Wold mechanism plus the moment condition.

Remark: Hilbert Embedding for SW⁡2\operatorname{SW}_2SW2​

In one dimension, W2\Wass_2W2​ is the L2(0,1)L^2(0,1)L2(0,1) distance between quantile functions. Hence

SW⁡2(α,β)2=∫Sd−1∫01∣Fθ,α−1(u)−Fθ,β−1(u)∣2 du dσ(θ).\operatorname{SW}_2(\alpha,\beta)^2 = \int_{\mathbb S^{d-1}}\int_0^1 \abs{F_{\theta,\alpha}^{-1}(u)-F_{\theta,\beta}^{-1}(u)}^2 \d u\d\sigma(\theta).SW2​(α,β)2=∫Sd−1​∫01​∣∣​Fθ,α−1​(u)−Fθ,β−1​(u)∣∣​2dudσ(θ).
(30)

Thus SW⁡2\operatorname{SW}_2SW2​ is a Hilbertian distance after embedding each measure into its field of projected quantiles. Consequently, exp⁡(−γSW⁡22)\exp(-\gamma\operatorname{SW}_2^2)exp(−γSW22​) is a positive definite kernel on probability measures for every γ>0\gamma>0γ>0.

Definition: Max-Sliced Wasserstein

The max-sliced distance replaces the average over directions by the most discriminating one:

MaxSW⁡p(α,β):=sup⁡θ∈Sd−1Wp((Pθ)♯α,(Pθ)♯β).\operatorname{MaxSW}_p(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \sup_{\theta\in\mathbb S^{d-1}} \Wass_p((P_\theta)_\sharp\alpha,(P_\theta)_\sharp\beta).MaxSWp​(α,β):=θ∈Sd−1sup​Wp​((Pθ​)♯​α,(Pθ​)♯​β).
(31)

Subspace-Sliced Variants¶

One-dimensional slices are extremely cheap, but they may discard too much geometry in high dimension. A natural compromise is to project onto kkk-dimensional subspaces: the projected OT problems remain lower dimensional, while each projection retains correlations inside a small block of coordinates.

Definition: Subspace-Sliced Wasserstein

Fix 1≤k≤d1\leq k\leq d1≤k≤d. Subspace-sliced variants replace one-dimensional lines by kkk-dimensional orthogonal projections. If U∈Rd×kU\in\RR^{d\times k}U∈Rd×k satisfies U⊤U=IkU^\top U=I_kU⊤U=Ik​, then

SW⁡p,k(α,β)p:=∫Wp((U⊤)♯α,(U⊤)♯β)p dU,\operatorname{SW}_{p,k}(\alpha,\beta)^p \eqdef \int \Wass_p((U^\top)_\sharp\alpha,(U^\top)_\sharp\beta)^p\d U,SWp,k​(α,β)p:=∫Wp​((U⊤)♯​α,(U⊤)♯​β)pdU,
(32)

where  dU\d UdU denotes the normalized invariant measure on the Stiefel manifold St⁡(d,k)={U∈Rd×k:U⊤U=Ik}\operatorname{St}(d,k)=\{U\in\RR^{d\times k}:U^\top U=I_k\}St(d,k)={U∈Rd×k:U⊤U=Ik​}, and

MaxSW⁡p,k(α,β):=sup⁡U⊤U=IkWp((U⊤)♯α,(U⊤)♯β).\operatorname{MaxSW}_{p,k}(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \sup_{U^\top U=I_k} \Wass_p((U^\top)_\sharp\alpha,(U^\top)_\sharp\beta).MaxSWp,k​(α,β):=U⊤U=Ik​sup​Wp​((U⊤)♯​α,(U⊤)♯​β).
(33)

The case k=1k=1k=1 recovers ordinary sliced and max-sliced Wasserstein, while k=dk=dk=d recovers the original Wasserstein distance.

Proposition: Basic Bounds for Sliced Variants

Let p≥1p\geq1p≥1 and let α,β∈Pp(Rd)\alpha,\beta\in\mathcal P_p(\RR^d)α,β∈Pp​(Rd). With normalized spherical and Stiefel measures,

SW⁡p(α,β)≤MaxSW⁡p(α,β)≤Wp(α,β).\operatorname{SW}_p(\alpha,\beta) \leq \operatorname{MaxSW}_p(\alpha,\beta) \leq \Wass_p(\alpha,\beta).SWp​(α,β)≤MaxSWp​(α,β)≤Wp​(α,β).
(34)

For kkk-dimensional subspace projections,

SW⁡p,k(α,β)≤MaxSW⁡p,k(α,β)≤Wp(α,β).\operatorname{SW}_{p,k}(\alpha,\beta) \leq \operatorname{MaxSW}_{p,k}(\alpha,\beta) \leq \Wass_p(\alpha,\beta).SWp,k​(α,β)≤MaxSWp,k​(α,β)≤Wp​(α,β).
(35)
Proof

The first inequality in each line follows because an LpL^pLp average over a probability space is bounded by the corresponding supremum. The second inequality follows because orthogonal projections are 1-Lipschitz: pushing any admissible coupling between α\alphaα and β\betaβ through a projection gives an admissible coupling for the projected measures with no larger transport cost. Optimizing over couplings and then averaging or maximizing over the projection gives the result.

Min-Sliced Lifted Transport Plans¶

The preceding constructions define distances between projected measures. A different use of slicing is to use a projection only as a device for building a feasible high-dimensional transport plan. For equal-weight empirical measures α=n−1∑iδxi\alpha=n^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{x_i}α=n−1∑i​δxi​​ and β=n−1∑iδyi\beta=n^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{y_i}β=n−1∑i​δyi​​, sort the projected samples ⟨xi,θ⟩\dotp{x_i}{\theta}⟨xi​,θ⟩ and ⟨yj,θ⟩\dotp{y_j}{\theta}⟨yj​,θ⟩, and let σθ\sigma_\thetaσθ​ be the monotone matching induced by this sorting. The lifted plan

πθ=1n∑i=1nδ(xi,yσθ(i))\pi_\theta = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \delta_{(x_i,y_{\sigma_\theta(i)})}πθ​=n1​i=1∑n​δ(xi​,yσθ​(i)​)​
(36)

is a genuine coupling between α\alphaα and β\betaβ in the original space. Min-SWGG-type methods then choose the projection whose lifted plan has the smallest full-dimensional quadratic cost:

MSWGG⁡2(α,β)2:=min⁡θ∈Sd−1∫∥x−y∥2 dπθ(x,y).\operatorname{MSWGG}_2(\alpha,\beta)^2 \eqdef \min_{\theta\in\mathbb S^{d-1}} \int\norm{x-y}^2\d\pi_\theta(x,y).MSWGG2​(α,β)2:=θ∈Sd−1min​∫∥x−y∥2dπθ​(x,y).
(37)

This quantity is not a projected distance; it is a cheap feasible-plan construction. Consequently,

W22(α,β)≤∫∥x−y∥2 dπθ(x,y),W22(α,β)≤MSWGG⁡2(α,β)2.\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta) \leq \int\norm{x-y}^2\d\pi_\theta(x,y), \qquad \Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta) \leq \operatorname{MSWGG}_2(\alpha,\beta)^2.W22​(α,β)≤∫∥x−y∥2dπθ​(x,y),W22​(α,β)≤MSWGG2​(α,β)2.
(38)
show_book_figure("min-sliced-transport-plan")

Lifted min-sliced plan. A one-dimensional direction is selected by a small deterministic sweep, then red and blue atoms are sorted after projection and matched in that order. The middle panel lifts this one-dimensional matching back to the plane; it is a feasible coupling but not the same object as the quadratic W2W_2W2​ matching shown on the right.

Vector Quantiles and Linear Optimal Transport¶

Linear OT starts from the multivariate analogue of quantile coordinates. The one-dimensional quantile function represents a probability measure by the monotone map sending a fixed reference law to it; in dimension d>1d>1d>1, Brenier’s theorem gives the corresponding construction after choosing an absolutely continuous reference probability ρ\rhoρ, typically the uniform law on a convex body or a standard Gaussian.

Vector Quantiles¶

Assume that ρ\rhoρ is absolutely continuous. For a target law μ\muμ with finite second moment, its vector quantile relative to ρ\rhoρ is the Brenier map

Tμ=∇ϕμ,(Tμ)♯ρ=μ,T_\mu=\nabla\phi_\mu, \qquad (T_\mu)_\sharp\rho=\mu,Tμ​=∇ϕμ​,(Tμ​)♯​ρ=μ,
(39)

or equivalently the solution of

min⁡T♯ρ=μ∫∥x−T(x)∥2 dρ(x).\min_{T_\sharp\rho=\mu} \int\norm{x-T(x)}^2\d\rho(x).T♯​ρ=μmin​∫∥x−T(x)∥2dρ(x).
(40)

This construction is canonical only after fixing ρ\rhoρ: changing the reference law changes the coordinates used to represent μ\muμ. Vector quantile regression uses the same idea conditionally, replacing scalar conditional quantiles by conditional Brenier maps and thereby encoding multivariate ranks and depths Carlier et al., 2017.

Linearized Wasserstein Coordinates¶

Linear OT replaces a nonlinear transport distance by a Hilbert norm between reference maps. It is useful when one reference measure is fixed and many nearby distributions must be compared cheaply. Let TαT_\alphaTα​ be the Brenier map pushing ρ\rhoρ to α\alphaα, understood as an element of L2(ρ;Rd)L^2(\rho;\RR^d)L2(ρ;Rd) and hence defined only ρ\rhoρ-almost everywhere. The linear OT embedding is

α↦Tα−Id∈L2(ρ;Rd),LOT⁡ρ(α,β)=∥Tα−Tβ∥L2(ρ).\alpha\mapsto T_\alpha-\Id\in L^2(\rho;\RR^d), \qquad \operatorname{LOT}_\rho(\alpha,\beta) = \norm{T_\alpha-T_\beta}_{L^2(\rho)}.α↦Tα​−Id∈L2(ρ;Rd),LOTρ​(α,β)=∥Tα​−Tβ​∥L2(ρ)​.
(41)

If one of the two targets equals the reference, the linearized distance is exact: for instance, LOT⁡ρ(ρ,α)=∥Tα−Id∥L2(ρ)=W2(ρ,α)\operatorname{LOT}_\rho(\rho,\alpha) =\norm{T_\alpha-\Id}_{L^2(\rho)} =\Wass_2(\rho,\alpha)LOTρ​(ρ,α)=∥Tα​−Id∥L2(ρ)​=W2​(ρ,α). For two arbitrary targets, the coupling (Tα,Tβ)♯ρ(T_\alpha,T_\beta)_\sharp\rho(Tα​,Tβ​)♯​ρ is admissible but not generally optimal, so LOT⁡ρ\operatorname{LOT}_\rhoLOTρ​ is a tangent-space approximation of the Wasserstein geometry Wang et al., 2013.

For a family (αs)s(\alpha_s)_s(αs​)s​ with weights (λs)s(\lambda_s)_s(λs​)s​, the linearized barycenter is obtained by averaging maps,

Tˉ=∑sλsTαs,αˉLOT⁡=Tˉ♯ρ.\bar T=\sum_s\lambda_s T_{\alpha_s}, \qquad \bar\alpha_{\operatorname{LOT}}=\bar T_\sharp\rho.Tˉ=s∑​λs​Tαs​​,αˉLOT​=Tˉ♯​ρ.
(42)

This is exact in one dimension, where quantile functions linearize W2\Wass_2W2​, and it is especially useful when many barycenters with changing weights must be evaluated quickly.

Remark: Three Hilbertian Embeddings of Measures

Kernel mean embeddings send α\alphaα to ∫k(x,⋅) dα(x)\int k(x,\cdot)\d\alpha(x)∫k(x,⋅)dα(x) in an RKHS and lead to MMD distances. Quadratic sliced Wasserstein sends a measure to the collection of one-dimensional quantile functions of its projections, viewed in L2(Sd−1×[0,1])L^2(\mathbb S^{d-1}\times[0,1])L2(Sd−1×[0,1]). Linear OT sends α\alphaα to the displacement field Tα−IdT_\alpha-\IdTα​−Id from a fixed reference ρ\rhoρ in L2(ρ;Rd)L^2(\rho;\RR^d)L2(ρ;Rd).

show_book_figure("dualnorms-linear-ot-embedding")

Linear OT coordinates. Fixing a reference measure ρ\rhoρ turns each target into a map TαT_\alphaTα​ from ρ\rhoρ to α\alphaα, or equivalently into the displacement field Tα−IdT_\alpha-\IdTα​−Id. In one dimension this is exactly the quantile parametrization of W2\Wass_2W2​. In two dimensions, averaging the maps gives the linearized barycenter, which is compared with the genuine McCann midpoint.

The next control keeps the exact one-dimensional setting. The reference density defines the coordinate system, the target maps are quantile maps from that reference, and the barycenter is obtained by averaging those maps before pushing the reference forward.

Interactive panel. Use the reference and deformation controls to inspect how linear optimal transport embeds measures through maps from a fixed template.

Proposition: Local Stability of Linear OT

Assume that the measures are supported on a fixed convex compact set, with densities bounded above and below, and that the Brenier maps from ρ\rhoρ are regular. Then, for α,β\alpha,\betaα,β in a sufficiently small regular neighborhood of ρ\rhoρ,

W2(α,β)≤LOT⁡ρ(α,β)andLOT⁡ρ(α,β)≤CW2(α,β)η\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta) \leq \operatorname{LOT}_\rho(\alpha,\beta) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \operatorname{LOT}_\rho(\alpha,\beta) \leq C\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta)^\etaW2​(α,β)≤LOTρ​(α,β)andLOTρ​(α,β)≤CW2​(α,β)η
(43)

for constants C>0C>0C>0 and η∈(0,1]\eta\in(0,1]η∈(0,1] depending on regularity.

Proof

The first inequality is immediate: (Tα,Tβ)♯ρ(T_\alpha,T_\beta)_\sharp\rho(Tα​,Tβ​)♯​ρ is a feasible coupling between α\alphaα and β\betaβ. The reverse local estimate is a standard stability statement for the Monge--Ampere equation under the stated regularity assumptions: changes in the target measure control changes in the Brenier potential in Holder norms, hence control Tα−TβT_\alpha-T_\betaTα​−Tβ​ in L2(ρ)L^2(\rho)L2(ρ).

In one-dimensional settings, quantile functions make this exact with η=1\eta=1η=1. In several dimensions one should not read the statement as a global Lipschitz estimate in W2\Wass_2W2​. Quantitative stability results for semi-discrete and Monge--Ampere maps give Holder exponents depending on the dimension, density bounds, support geometry and regularity Mérigot et al., 2020.

Spectral and Robust Wasserstein Distances¶

Spectral OT changes the scalar quadratic cost by measuring the whole displacement covariance through a matrix gauge. The same object admits a robust projected formulation: instead of fixing one projection, one maximizes over the polar set of the gauge. Subspace robust OT is the important non-convex rank-constrained version of this idea Paty & Cuturi, 2019; spectral gauges provide its convex minimax counterpart and connect to recent spectral-gradient viewpoints such as Muon dynamics Peyré, 2026.

Definition: Monotone Spectral Gauge

A monotone spectral gauge on positive semidefinite matrices is a convex, positively 1-homogeneous map γ:S+d→R+\gamma:\mathbb S_+^d\to\RR_+γ:S+d​→R+​ such that γ(M)=0\gamma(M)=0γ(M)=0 only for M=0M=0M=0, γ(QMQ⊤)=γ(M)\gamma(QMQ^\top)=\gamma(M)γ(QMQ⊤)=γ(M) for every orthogonal matrix QQQ, and

0⪯M⪯N⟹γ(M)≤γ(N).0\preceq M\preceq N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \gamma(M)\leq\gamma(N).0⪯M⪯N⟹γ(M)≤γ(N).
(44)

The monotonicity condition means that increasing the displacement covariance in Loewner order cannot decrease the transport penalty.

Definition: Spectral Wasserstein Distance

Let γ\gammaγ be a monotone spectral gauge. For a coupling π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β), define its displacement covariance

Mπ:=∫(x−y)(x−y)⊤ dπ(x,y).M_\pi \eqdef \int (x-y)(x-y)^\top\d\pi(x,y).Mπ​:=∫(x−y)(x−y)⊤dπ(x,y).
(45)

The spectral Wasserstein distance associated with γ\gammaγ is

Wγ(α,β)2:=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)γ(Mπ).\Wass_\gamma(\alpha,\beta)^2 \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \gamma(M_\pi).Wγ​(α,β)2:=π∈Π(α,β)inf​γ(Mπ​).
(46)

The special case γ(M)=tr⁡(M)\gamma(M)=\tr(M)γ(M)=tr(M) gives the usual quadratic Wasserstein distance W2\Wass_2W2​. The spectral gauge γ(M)=λmax⁡(M)\gamma(M)=\lambda_{\max}(M)γ(M)=λmax​(M) instead measures the worst transported variance direction. For A⪰0A\succeq0A⪰0, define the quadratic projected transport cost

W2,A(α,β)2:=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫(x−y)⊤A(x−y) dπ(x,y)=W2((A1/2)♯α,(A1/2)♯β)2.\Wass_{2,A}(\alpha,\beta)^2 \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int (x-y)^\top A(x-y)\d\pi(x,y) = \Wass_2((A^{1/2})_\sharp\alpha,(A^{1/2})_\sharp\beta)^2.W2,A​(α,β)2:=π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫(x−y)⊤A(x−y)dπ(x,y)=W2​((A1/2)♯​α,(A1/2)♯​β)2.
(47)

The polar set of the gauge is

Bγ:={A⪰0:tr⁡(AM)≤γ(M) for all M⪰0},\mathcal B_\gamma \eqdef \{A\succeq0: \tr(AM)\leq\gamma(M)\ \text{for all } M\succeq0\},Bγ​:={A⪰0:tr(AM)≤γ(M) for all M⪰0},
(48)

so that, for a closed gauge, γ(M)=sup⁡A∈Bγtr⁡(AM)\gamma(M)=\sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma}\tr(AM)γ(M)=supA∈Bγ​​tr(AM).

Proposition: Robust Representation and Metric Equivalence

Assume, for simplicity, that the measures are compactly supported and that γ\gammaγ is closed and finite on the positive semidefinite cone. Then

Wγ(α,β)2=sup⁡A∈BγW2,A(α,β)2.\Wass_\gamma(\alpha,\beta)^2 = \sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma} \Wass_{2,A}(\alpha,\beta)^2.Wγ​(α,β)2=A∈Bγ​sup​W2,A​(α,β)2.
(49)

If there exist constants 0<a≤b<+∞0<a\leq b<+\infty0<a≤b<+∞ such that aI∈BγaI\in\mathcal B_\gammaaI∈Bγ​ and Bγ⊂{A:0⪯A⪯bI}\mathcal B_\gamma\subset\{A:0\preceq A\preceq bI\}Bγ​⊂{A:0⪯A⪯bI}, equivalently

atr⁡(M)≤γ(M)≤btr⁡(M)(M⪰0),a\tr(M)\leq\gamma(M)\leq b\tr(M) \qquad (M\succeq0),atr(M)≤γ(M)≤btr(M)(M⪰0),
(50)

then

a W2(α,β)≤Wγ(α,β)≤b W2(α,β).\sqrt a\,\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta) \leq \Wass_\gamma(\alpha,\beta) \leq \sqrt b\,\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta).a​W2​(α,β)≤Wγ​(α,β)≤b​W2​(α,β).
(51)

In particular, Wγ\Wass_\gammaWγ​ is a distance.

Proof

Using the polar representation of γ\gammaγ,

Wγ(α,β)2=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)sup⁡A∈Bγtr⁡(AMπ).\Wass_\gamma(\alpha,\beta)^2 = \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma} \tr(AM_\pi).Wγ​(α,β)2=π∈Π(α,β)inf​A∈Bγ​sup​tr(AMπ​).
(52)

The coupling set is convex and compact for weak convergence under compact support. The polar set Bγ\mathcal B_\gammaBγ​ is convex and compact, and the map (π,A)↦tr⁡(AMπ)(\pi,A)\mapsto\tr(AM_\pi)(π,A)↦tr(AMπ​) is affine in each variable and continuous. Sion’s minimax theorem gives

inf⁡πsup⁡A∈Bγtr⁡(AMπ)=sup⁡A∈Bγinf⁡πtr⁡(AMπ)=sup⁡A∈BγW2,A(α,β)2.\inf_\pi\sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma}\tr(AM_\pi) = \sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma}\inf_\pi\tr(AM_\pi) = \sup_{A\in\mathcal B_\gamma}\Wass_{2,A}(\alpha,\beta)^2.πinf​A∈Bγ​sup​tr(AMπ​)=A∈Bγ​sup​πinf​tr(AMπ​)=A∈Bγ​sup​W2,A​(α,β)2.
(53)

For fixed A⪰0A\succeq0A⪰0, W2,A\Wass_{2,A}W2,A​ is the Wasserstein pseudodistance associated with the seminorm x↦∥A1/2x∥x\mapsto\norm{A^{1/2}x}x↦∥∥​A1/2x∥∥​. A supremum of pseudodistances is symmetric and satisfies the triangle inequality. If aI∈BγaI\in\mathcal B_\gammaaI∈Bγ​ and A⪯bIA\preceq bIA⪯bI for all A∈BγA\in\mathcal B_\gammaA∈Bγ​, then

aW2(α,β)2≤Wγ(α,β)2≤bW2(α,β)2,a\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta)^2 \leq \Wass_\gamma(\alpha,\beta)^2 \leq b\Wass_2(\alpha,\beta)^2,aW2​(α,β)2≤Wγ​(α,β)2≤bW2​(α,β)2,
(54)

which proves definiteness and equivalence with W2\Wass_2W2​.

Definition: Subspace Robust Wasserstein

For 1≤k≤d1\leq k\leq d1≤k≤d, the Paty--Cuturi subspace robust Wasserstein distance is

SRW⁡2,k(α,β):=sup⁡U⊤U=IkW2((U⊤)♯α,(U⊤)♯β)=sup⁡P2=P=P⊤, tr⁡(P)=kW2,P(α,β).\operatorname{SRW}_{2,k}(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \sup_{U^\top U=I_k} \Wass_2((U^\top)_\sharp\alpha,(U^\top)_\sharp\beta) = \sup_{P^2=P=P^\top,\ \tr(P)=k} \Wass_{2,P}(\alpha,\beta).SRW2,k​(α,β):=U⊤U=Ik​sup​W2​((U⊤)♯​α,(U⊤)♯​β)=P2=P=P⊤, tr(P)=ksup​W2,P​(α,β).
(55)

For the Ky Fan gauge

γk(M)=∑ℓ=1kλℓ(M),\gamma_k(M)=\sum_{\ell=1}^k\lambda_\ell(M),γk​(M)=ℓ=1∑k​λℓ​(M),
(56)

where the eigenvalues are sorted in decreasing order, the polar set is

Bγk={A:0⪯A⪯I, tr⁡(A)≤k}.\mathcal B_{\gamma_k} = \{A:0\preceq A\preceq I,\ \tr(A)\leq k\}.Bγk​​={A:0⪯A⪯I, tr(A)≤k}.
(57)

Thus k=dk=dk=d gives γd(M)=tr⁡(M)\gamma_d(M)=\tr(M)γd​(M)=tr(M) and recovers W2\Wass_2W2​. The convex hull of rank-kkk projectors is

{A:0⪯A⪯I, tr⁡(A)=k},\{A:0\preceq A\preceq I,\ \tr(A)=k\},{A:0⪯A⪯I, tr(A)=k},
(58)

and, since M⪰0M\succeq0M⪰0, the associated support function is the same Ky Fan gauge. Thus Wγk\Wass_{\gamma_k}Wγk​​ is the convexified spectral counterpart of SRW⁡2,k\operatorname{SRW}_{2,k}SRW2,k​, while SRW⁡2,k\operatorname{SRW}_{2,k}SRW2,k​ keeps the original non-convex rank constraint. For k=1k=1k=1, γ1(M)=λmax⁡(M)\gamma_1(M)=\lambda_{\max}(M)γ1​(M)=λmax​(M) and Bγ1={A⪰0:tr⁡(A)≤1}\mathcal B_{\gamma_1}=\{A\succeq0:\tr(A)\leq1\}Bγ1​​={A⪰0:tr(A)≤1}.

show_book_figure("spectral-wasserstein-gauge")

Trace and spectral gauges for displacement covariances. The trace gauge minimizes the average squared displacement and gives the usual quadratic transport plan. The λmax⁡\lambda_{\max}λmax​ gauge penalizes the worst projected displacement variance; the displayed plan is obtained by approximating the robust formulation with finitely many directions.

The interactive demo turns the displacement covariance into a visible object. The trace gauge sums both covariance eigenvalues, while the top-eigenvalue gauge cares only about the worst transported direction.

Interactive panel. Use the spectral weights and deformation controls to see how the gauge changes the geometry used to compare measures.

References¶
  1. Liero, M., Mielke, A., & Savaré, G. (2018). Optimal entropy-transport problems and a new Hellinger–Kantorovich distance between positive measures. Inventiones Mathematicae, 211(3), 969–1117.
  2. Chizat, L., Peyré, G., Schmitzer, B., & Vialard, F.-X. (2018). Unbalanced optimal transport: dynamic and Kantorovich formulation. Journal of Functional Analysis, 274(11), 3090–3123.
  3. Chizat, L., Schmitzer, B., Peyré, G., & Vialard, F.-X. (2018). An interpolating distance between optimal transport and Fisher–Rao metrics. Foundations of Computational Mathematics, 18(1), 1–44.
  4. Rabin, J., Peyré, G., Delon, J., & Bernot, M. (2011). Wasserstein barycenter and its application to texture mixing. International Conference on Scale Space and Variational Methods in Computer Vision, 435–446.
  5. Bonneel, N., Rabin, J., Peyré, G., & Pfister, H. (2015). Sliced and Radon Wasserstein barycenters of measures. Journal of Mathematical Imaging and Vision, 51(1), 22–45.
  6. Kolouri, S., Zou, Y., & Rohde, G. K. (2016). Sliced Wasserstein kernels for probability distributions. Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 5258–5267.
  7. Nadjahi, K., Durmus, A., Simsekli, U., & Badeau, R. (2019). Asymptotic Guarantees for Learning Generative Models with the Sliced-Wasserstein Distance. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems.
  8. Carlier, G., Chernozhukov, V., & Galichon, A. (2017). Vector quantile regression beyond the specified case. Journal of Multivariate Analysis, 161, 96–102. 10.1016/j.jmva.2017.07.003
  9. Wang, W., Slepčev, D., Basu, S., Ozolek, J. A., & Rohde, G. K. (2013). A linear optimal transportation framework for quantifying and visualizing variations in sets of images. International Journal of Computer Vision, 101(2), 254–269.
  10. Mérigot, Q., Delalande, A., & Chazal, F. (2020). Quantitative Stability of Optimal Transport Maps and Linearization of the 2-Wasserstein Space. Proceedings of the Twenty Third International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, 108, 3186–3196.
  11. Paty, F.-P., & Cuturi, M. (2019). Subspace Robust Wasserstein Distances. Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning, 97, 5072–5081.
  12. Peyré, G. (2026). Muon Dynamics as a Spectral Wasserstein Flow. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2604.04891.
